name: Neon
information_for_contributors: Derived from the compiler, not from the docs: compiler/src/lexer/token.rs for the
reserved words and the punctuation alphabet, compiler/src/lexer/mod.rs for literal
forms and nesting comments, compiler/src/expand.rs `lookup()` for the annotation
names, and compiler/src/ops.rs for which words are binary operators.
ORDERING IS LOAD-BEARING. TextMate tries the patterns of a rule list in order at
each position and the first that matches wins, so #declarations must precede the
generic #properties rule (otherwise `let x: i64` colours `x` as a record field),
#namespace must precede #type-names (so `List` in `List::new` reads as a module
rather than a type, matching the tree-sitter query), and #comments stays first so
the `/` operator never swallows the `//` that opens a line comment.
WHAT THIS FILE STRUCTURALLY CANNOT DO, and what does it instead:
extra/tree-sitter-neon has a real parser, so its highlights.scm answers all of the
following from the tree. A regular expression cannot, at any level of effort:
* Tell a type from a value. `entity.name.type` here is the `[A-Z]` convention and
nothing more. A lowercase type alias renders as a variable; a constant named
`MAX` renders as a type. The grammar knows because it knows type position.
* Tell a record literal from a block. `Point { x: 1 }` versus `if c { x }` differ
only by what precedes the brace, arbitrarily far back.
* Tell type parameters from a turbofish from an index: `[T]`, `f[T](x)` and
`xs[i]` are the same three tokens. Handled here only where a declaration
keyword makes the position unambiguous within one rule -- see #type-parameters,
which covers `fn` and the type declarations and nothing else.
* Resolve a name to its binding. No shadowing, no scopes, no local go-to-def, no
distinction between a definition and a use. locals.scm does all four.
* Count. Nesting is faked with recursive begin/end rules (see #block-comment and
#interpolation-braces), which works but costs a rule per nesting construct.
Semantic tokens would close most of this gap without a grammar, but neon-lsp does
not advertise `semanticTokensProvider` (see lsp/src/main.rs), so the heuristics
below are the honest ceiling for VS Code today.

patterns.comment: The six annotations the compiler recognises: `lookup()` in compiler/src/expand.rs is the complete registry. `inline` was added after this file was written and is the reason to check lookup() rather than trusting this comment. Any argument that follows is left to the ordinary string and punctuation rules.
An unrecognised annotation. The compiler rejects it; highlight it as an annotation anyway so the mistake is visible in context.

interpolation-braces.comment: A record literal inside a hole must find its own closing brace before the hole's.

patterns.comment: \# escapes an interpolation, so \#{ is a literal #{.
patterns.name: constant.character.escape.neon

patterns.comment: `is_float` in compiler/src/lexer/mod.rs is a dot followed by a digit, OR an `e`/`E` — so the fraction and the exponent are alternatives here and NOT both optional. They used to both be optional, which made the whole trailing group vacuous and matched a bare `1`: every integer in every Neon file was scoped constant.numeric.float. Requiring the dot keeps `x.0` field access and the `xs..1` spread out of it, which is exactly why the lexer requires it too.
patterns.name: constant.numeric.float.neon

atoms.comment: `:name` is an atom only when the colon does not directly follow an identifier — that look-back is what keeps `{ x:y }` from meaning something different to `{ x: y }`. The leading `:` exclusion keeps `::` out.
atoms.name: constant.other.atom.neon

patterns.comment: `orphan` is CONTEXTUAL, not reserved. It is absent from Token::keyword in compiler/src/lexer/token.rs; the parser matches it as a bare identifier immediately before `impl` (parser/mod.rs:502, `ident_named("orphan")`). So it is matched with that lookahead rather than added to the keyword list — `let orphan = 1` is legal Neon and must stay a variable. It rendered as variable.other everywhere before this rule existed.

A function declaration is begin/end rather than a bare match so that the signature can be a scope of its own: inside it, `[T]` is type parameters and `name:` is a parameter, neither of which is decidable from a lone regex anywhere else in the file. It closes before the body brace, at a `;` (an @native fn has no body), or at end of line — the last is the safety net that stops a malformed signature from swallowing the file. A signature wrapped across lines is still safe, because #parameter-list is an active begin/end block while it is open and the outer end is not tested inside it.
mu type A = ... — an explicit recursive-type binder.
`mod` names a module, not a type: the tree-sitter query captures it as @module and this now matches. Split out of the rule below, where it had been sharing entity.name.type with `record` and friends.

opaque record / internal protocol, and the plain forms. begin/end for the same reason `fn` is: it opens a scope in which `[T]` is unambiguously type parameters. It ends at the body brace, the `=` of a type alias, a `;`, or end of line.
`impl [T] P for T` — the optional bracket group is type parameters, so this is begin/end too. It ends at the brace that opens the method list.
Consumes the bound name so the generic #properties rule never sees it — otherwise the `x` of `let x: i64` would colour as a record field.

type-parameters.comment: `[T]`, `[T, U]`, `[T: Display + Ord]`. Two anchors make this decidable, and both are needed. It is only reachable from inside a declaration head, where a `[` cannot be an index; and `\G` pins it to the position immediately after the declaration name, which is the only place a *binding* bracket group can appear. Without the `\G` this also fired on type ARGUMENTS later in the same signature — `fn range(...) -> List[i64]` coloured `i64` as a type parameter. A parameter within the group is then recognised by position (first, or after a comma) rather than by shape, because `[T: Display]` ends with a name too and matching by shape would colour the bound as a parameter.

parameter-list.comment: A parameter is `name: type` (parser/mod.rs: `parameter`), with no defaults, so a name followed by a single colon is unambiguous inside these parentheses. #nested-parens comes first and must: a parenthesised function type in a parameter's type, `fn f(g: (i64) -> str)`, would otherwise let the inner `)` close this rule early.

patterns.comment: `orelse` is deliberately NOT here. It reads like exception control flow but compiler/src/ops.rs makes it a binary operator — `BinOpInfo { op: BinOp::Orelse, prec: 1, text: "orelse" }`, the lowest rung of the one precedence ladder the parser and formatter share. It is scoped with the other word operators below, which is where the tree-sitter grammar puts it too.
patterns.name: keyword.control.exception.neon

patterns.comment: Operators that are words, per the table in compiler/src/ops.rs. `and` binds tighter than `or`; `orelse` is the lowest rung of all, below `or`.
patterns.name: keyword.operator.word.neon

patterns.comment: `enum` is deliberately not a keyword — sum types are unions of records — but the parser reports a dedicated diagnostic when it is used as a declaration, so flag it.
patterns.name: invalid.illegal.not-a-keyword.neon

builtin-types.comment: The base types, answered by the checker rather than declared in a prelude, plus `any`, which is syntax.
builtin-types.name: support.type.builtin.neon

namespace.comment: A leading segment of a path: the `std` and `io` of `std::io::println`. Placed before #type-names on purpose, so the `List` of `List::new` reads as a module rather than a type — which is what highlights.scm does with `(path (identifier) @module (identifier))`. Only segments FOLLOWED by `::` match, so the final segment falls through to the type, call or identifier rules as appropriate.
namespace.name: entity.name.namespace.neon

type-names.comment: A heuristic, and the biggest single gap against the tree-sitter grammar: an initial capital reads as a type or a record constructor, and that is all the information available. neon-lsp advertises no semanticTokensProvider (lsp/src/main.rs), so nothing can refine it from outside either. A lowercase type alias is missed; a SCREAMING_CASE constant is a false positive.
type-names.name: entity.name.type.neon

field-access.comment: `p.x`. The lookbehind excludes a second dot so the `..` of a record or list spread never opens a field, and #numbers has already run, so the `.5` of a float is gone before this is reached. There is no method-call form in Neon — a dot is always field access (which is why neon-lsp leaves `.` out of its completion triggers) — so no call lookahead is needed.

properties.comment: A name bound to a single colon: a field declaration in a record, a field initializer in a record literal, or a field in a record pattern. `x: i64` in a `let` never reaches here because #declarations consumed the name first, and a parameter never reaches here because #parameter-list scoped it first. The `(?!:)` keeps `::` out. Known miss, inherent to the approach: a lambda parameter written `|x: i64|` is a parameter and gets coloured as a property, because nothing distinguishes the two positions without a parse.

patterns.comment: `..` is record and list spread. There is no `..=`.
patterns.name: keyword.operator.spread.neon
